coredata.dc:title: Experimental and Molecular Dynamic Modeling Studies of Electrospun Carbon Fiber Electrode Performance Enhancement by Potassium Ferricyanide Addition
coredata.dc:creator: Lamiel, Charmaine SJ.
Afroz, Mohammad M.
Cincotta, Robert E.F.
Krödel, Maximillian
Li-Oakey, Katie D.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we convert coal extraction waste into functional carbon fiber electrodes. Detailed experimental and modeling studies reveal the importance of surface properties and dramatic increase (162%) in capacitance with adding small amount of 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6 to 6 M KOH (referred to as mixed electrolyte). Molecular dynamics (MD) modeling reveals that ferricyanide ions in the mixed electrolyte contributed to the increase of both pseudocapacitance and electric double layer capacitance. With the mixed electrolyte, a symmetric supercapacitor (SSC) using coal-derived carbon fiber mat electrodes can deliver energy density as high as16.8 Wh kg–1 at a power density of 362.2 W kg–1, with excellent cycling stability (∼95.83% at 10,000 cycles). This study provides an insightful perspective on the strategic efforts of nonthermal usage of coal as we transition to renewable energy economy. Additionally, the mixed electrolyte effect and MD modeling results shed light on carbon electrodes from biomass, such as lignin, for various electrochemical energy devices.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical characterization of CNFs in a three-electrode system
The mixed electrolyte (6 M KOH + 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6 electrolyte)
Comparison between the two electrolytes coupled with theoretical simulations

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: University of Wyoming School of Energy Resources

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Mechanical and Process Engineering

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We sincerely thank School of Energy Resources (SER) at University of Wyoming for funding this project and Professors Joseph Holles at Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Wyoming , and Christoph Müller at Laboratory of Energy Science and Engineering, Department of Mechanical and Process Engineering, ETH, Zürich , for allowing us to use their physisorption instruments to characterize CNF porous structures.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface Characterization of powder river basin (PRB) coal and coal residue from tetralin-extraction, referred to as TR: (a) FTIR and (b) XRD of raw powders.
(a) SEM image, (b) adsorption/desorption isotherms of nitrogen at 77 K, and (c) pore volume over pore size distribution for representative PAN-PVP-20TR carbon nanofiber (CNF) mat.

Optical images (a, c, e, g, i) of the as-spun fiber mats on Al foil at 4.5 times magnification and their corresponding SEM images (b, d, f, h, j) after carbonization, with the arrow in j indicating the flakes of TR for PAN-PVP-50TR.
(a) CV curves at 50 mV s–1, (b) Nyquist plot, (c) specific capacitance as a function of current density, and (d) specific capacitance at 1 A g–1 of PAN-PVP-TR fiber samples in 6 M KOH electrolyte.

(a) CV curves at 50 mV s–1, (b) Nyquist plot (c) specific capacitance as a function of current density, and (d) specific capacitances at 1 A g–1 of as-prepared PAN-PVP-TR samples in 6 M KOH + 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6 electrolyte.

Comparison of the electrochemical performance of PAN-PVP-20TR in 6 M KOH and 6 M KOH + 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6: (a) CV curves at 50 mV s–1, (b) GCD profile at 1 A g–1, (c) replotted GCD curves of (b) to highlight the difference in ratio of (discharge time)/(charge time) in 6 M KOH (1.57) and 6 M KOH + 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6 (2.06), capacitive distribution in (d) 6 M KOH and (e) 6 M KOH + 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6, and (f) Nyquist plots.

Ion distribution versus distance on (a, c) positive and (b, d) negative electrode side in (a-b) 6 M KOH and (c-d) mixed electrolyte (6 M KOH + 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6) under 1.0 V applied potential. x1: Stern layer thickness; x2: diffuse layer thickness.; xf: modified Stern layer thickness. Comparison of ion distribution between 6 M KOH (solid line) and the mixed (dashed line) electrolyte for (e) K+ (f) OH– at the positive electrode, indicating that the ion distribution is shifted away from the electrode in the mixed electrolyte, and (g) K+ and (h) OH– at the negative electrode, revealing that the ion distribution did not shift in the mixed electrolyte. Schematic illustration of ion adsorption on the (i) positive electrode showing a thicker diffuse layer in the mixed electrolyte, and (j) negative electrode showing the same diffuse layer thickness.

(a) CV curves at 50 mV s–1 and (b) GCD at 1 A g–1 and varied potential window, and (c) GCD at varied current densities at fixed 1.6 V. (d) Ragone plot and (e) 10,000 cycle performance based on PAN-PVP-20TR SSC device. (f) Application of SSC device in lighting 10-parallel blue and red LEDs.

head.ce:title: Experimental and Molecular Dynamic Modeling Studies of Electrospun Carbon Fiber Electrode Performance Enhancement by Potassium Ferricyanide Addition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Wyoming, Laramie, WY 82070, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemical Engineering, University of Wyoming, Laramie, WY 82070, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Process Engineering, ETH Zürich, 8092 Zürich, Switzerland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Process Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Mechanical and Process Engineering, ETH Zürich, 8092 Zürich, Switzerland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we convert coal extraction waste into functional carbon fiber electrodes. Detailed experimental and modeling studies reveal the importance of surface properties and dramatic increase (162%) in capacitance with adding small amount of 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6 to 6 M KOH (referred to as mixed electrolyte). Molecular dynamics (MD) modeling reveals that ferricyanide ions in the mixed electrolyte contributed to the increase of both pseudocapacitance and electric double layer capacitance. With the mixed electrolyte, a symmetric supercapacitor (SSC) using coal-derived carbon fiber mat electrodes can deliver energy density as high as16.8 Wh kg–1 at a power density of 362.2 W kg–1, with excellent cycling stability (∼95.83% at 10,000 cycles). This study provides an insightful perspective on the strategic efforts of nonthermal usage of coal as we transition to renewable energy economy. Additionally, the mixed electrolyte effect and MD modeling results shed light on carbon electrodes from biomass, such as lignin, for various electrochemical energy devices.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: From electricity generation and petroleum production, coal and crude oil are massive providers of the world's energy demand and petroleum by-products, respectively. While it produces valuable commodities, CO2 emissions from coal-based power plants also contribute to global warming and climate change. However, abruptly cutting our dependence on coal will risk a global energy shortage. Thus, federal and state governments have been providing resources for relevant studies in laws, policies, and subsidies that enable the gradual elimination of coal as a thermal source by advocating its nonthermal utilization, including feasible pathways of converting coal to energy storage devices through coal refining using solvent extraction, which is a crucial component during the energy transition from fossil fuels to renewable resources. Additionally, the nonthermal utilization of coal, especially as carbon electrodes, can serve as a crucial connecting piece during the energy transition thanks to the existing production and transportation infrastructure built over the past decades [1–4]. Currently, during the thermal conversion of burning coal for energy production, coal dust, and coal fly ash are usually discarded and disposed of through landfills [5]. Repurposing these coal components has paved the way for research directions toward nonthermal use of coal, including converting coal mining waste for brick fabrication [6], extracting valuable critical metal, and synthesizing 3D aluminosilicate and carbon-based materials from coal fly ash [7].

In the last two decades, there has been a growing interest in converting coal for energy storage such as batteries and supercapacitors [8]. Particularly, coal-derived carbon materials [9] are utilized as effective materials for an electrical double-layer capacitor (EDLC)-based electrodes. For EDLCs, the storage mechanism relies on the large surface area that promotes electrostatic (non-Faradaic) charge storage at the electrode-electrolyte interface [10]. Structure modification and surface functionalization techniques are essential to prepare coal-based materials with high surface area, desirable porous structure, excellent conductivity, and high thermal stability, which are hallmarks of an efficient electrode. Recently, one-dimensional (1D) structures, such as nanoneedle [11], nanowire [12], nanotube [13], nanorod [14], and nanofiber [15], have shown exceptional properties as battery and supercapacitor electrodes. For instance, 1D carbon nanofibers (CNFs) grown via chemical vapor deposition (CVD) have shown high surface area, unique porous structure, and high conductivity, which, in turn, demonstrate reasonable capacitance from the electronic double layer [16]. In addition to exploring structure effect [17,18], capacitance enhancement has been demonstrated vis preparation of hybrid nanocomposites [19], porous architectures [20], and redox-active electrolytes [21,22].

The electrospinning technique has proven to be an effective process for producing large-scale 1D CNFs. A wide range of polymers are often used as precursors for electrospinning owing to their physiochemical properties, such as polymer's molecular weight, and polymer conductivity, which will dictate the physiochemical properties of the resultant electrospinning solution, including viscosity, surface tension, and conductivity [23]. Various polymers have been spun into nanofibers including natural polymers (e.g. silk, chitosan, collagen, gelatin, fibrinogen, alginate, polysaccharide, hyaluronic acid, etc.), and synthetic polymers (e.g. polyurethane, polyacrylonitrile (PAN), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), polyvinyl alcohol, polymethacrylate, polyethylene oxide, etc.) [24]. In this study, PAN is chosen as a polymer binder due to its high carbon yield, high strength-to-weight, and high stiffness-to-weight properties after carbonization [25]. PVP is chosen as a porogen because it not only induces pores in polymer-based carbon fibers but also acts as a stabilizing agent to prevent aggregation caused by the integration of other particles [26]. Using the combination of PAN/PVP in developing CNFs has been widely investigated and evaluated as effective carbon electrodes [27–29]. The unique contribution of this study focuses on a new carbon source and the effect of adding 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6 to commonly used 6 M KOH electrolyte.

Previous study by our group and others have used solvent-extracted coal fragments as carbon source electrospinning process. These reports clearly demonstrated that different solvents, such as nonpolar solvents [30], acid [31], and ionic liquids [29], and corresponding solvent treatment time and temperature generated coal fragments that possess dramatically different physiochemical properties, which consequently may affect the physiochemical properties of the final carbon fiber mat and electrochemical storage capacity. In this study, a waste product, often referred to as coal residue, from a cost-effective solvent (tetralin) extraction process, is used as a cheap carbon source [30]. Based on previous work from our group, incorporating coal extract in the electrospinning process has produced CNFs with competitive electrode performance (299.4 F g–1), compared to that from commercial PAN alone (124.3 F g–1), or that from the PAN/PVP pair (157.8 F g–1) [29]. Herein, we use tetralin-extracted coal residue, a waste stream, (referred to as TR for simplicity hereafter) for the electrospinning solution and evaluated its effect on physiochemical properties of final CNFs. The CNF electrodes were evaluated in the basic electrolyte, KOH, and with an electrolyte additive, K3Fe(CN)6. Our molecular modeling study shows that the addition of K3Fe(CN)6 contributes to both electrical double-layers and supplemental redox activities [32–34]. Using the TR-CNF as our carbon electrodes, this paper further probes the EDL and performance enhancement of adding K3Fe(CN)6 experimentally and theoretically via molecular dynamic modeling. Symmetrical supercapacitors (SSC) showed significantly increased capacitance performance (162%) in the mixed electrolyte, compared to those tested with only 6M KOH.

Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) (150,000 g mol–1) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) (360,000 g mol–1) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8%) (Sigma-Aldrich) was used as the solvent for the spinnable solution. Nickel foam (1.6 mm thickness) was purchased from MTI Corporation. For the precipitation test, deionized water (DI water (ASTM Type II, Reagent ACS), ethanol (anhydrous, Decon Labs, Inc.), acetone (ACS, Fisher Chemical), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, ACS, Macron Fine Chemicals) were used. All chemicals were used as received and no further treatments were done.

The coal residue was the waste product from the coal extraction process as described by Holberg et al. [30]. Subbituminous coal was acquired from the Cordero Rojo Mine (part of the Powder River Basin, or PRB) in Gillette, WY, USA. Briefly, PRB coal was crushed to achieve a particle size of 0.25 – 1.65 mm. The crushed coal particles were mixed with 1,2,3,4-tetrahydronaphthalene (tetralin) in a pressurized cell in the previous study [30]. The cell was heated at a fixed temperature of 360°C and a pressure of 1.7 MPa, with a tetralin feed flow rate of 0.1 mL min–1 per gram of PRB coal. After the thermal treatment, the residue that was not dissolved in tetralin at 360°C was filtered and dried in situ under flowing argon at 250°C for about 24 hours. The resulting product is named tetralin extracted coal residue (referred to as TR), which is used as the feedstock for the electrospinning experiment in this study.

The spinning solutions were prepared as shown in Table S1. First, the TR powder was ball-milled and sieved to particle size of less than 250 µm. Then, a varied amount of TR powder was dispersed in DMF. The solution was heated at 100°C and mixed thoroughly until a homogeneous suspension was ready. Built upon our previous work [29], the PAN/PVP pair was used as our polymer binder and porogen. Specifically, a fixed amount of PAN and PVP with a weight ratio of 0.5 g to 0.5 g per 9 g of DMF (10% polymer by weight) was used as the baseline solution recipe. After sifting, TR powder with particle size of < 250 µm was used in making electrospinning solution. Solutions with different amounts of TR were made as detailed in Table S1. The TR powder percentage was calculated based on the dry weight of PAN, PVP and TR. For example, PAN-PVP-100TR contains a total of 1 g polymer (0.5 g of PAN and 0.5 g PVP) and 1 g TR. After stirring overnight, the solution contained TR particles dispersed homogeneously in the polymeric solution. It was cooled to room temperature before the electrospinning process.

A 3-mL syringe (diameter of 9.43 mm) was filled with the spinning solution after cooling it to room temperature. Then, the needle (0.08 mm inner diameter) and the syringe were connected through a hose (40 cm in length). The distance of the needle to the rotating drum collector was kept at 15 cm. The rotating drum (18 cm in width, 9 cm in diameter) was covered with commercial aluminum foil which was used for collecting the as-spun fibers. The needle had an applied voltage of 20 kV, and the rotating drum was kept at 0 kV while running at 800 rpm. Throughout the electrospinning process, the climate was maintained at 30% relative humidity and 30°C temperature, and the flow rate of the syringe was kept at 0.7 mL h–1.

After the electrospinning process was finished, the commercial aluminum foil was detached from the rotating drum. With the use of tweezers, the as-spun fiber mat was carefully peeled from the foil. The as-spun fiber mat was laid on a Teflon-lined baking pan, sealed with aluminum foil, and transferred to a muffle oven. The as-spun fiber mat was oxidized at 300°C for 3 h with a ramp profile of 1°C per min. Upon cooling naturally to room temperature, carbonization of the as-oxidized fiber mat followed. The carbonization process was carried out at 800°C (ramp rate of 5°C per min) for one hour under argon. The carbonized carbon nanofiber mat (CNF) was retrieved after cooling naturally at room temperature.

The thermal stability of the powder samples was analyzed by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) using TGA 5500 (TA Instruments). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrums were recorded in the 400-4000 cm–1 range by using Nicolet iS50 (Thermo Scientific) with an iS50 ABX automated beam splitter exchanger. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of PRB coal and TR powder were obtained using a Rigaku Model RU300 instrument using Cu Kα radiation, with a power setting of 44 kV and 44 mA. Nitrogen adsorption measurements were carried out using a NOVA 4000e Analyzer by Quantachrome over a relative pressure range of 0.001–0.99 p/p0 with p0 = 1 atm. The samples were initially degassed at 250°C for 3 h under vacuum. The surface area was calculated using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) model and the pore size distribution was evaluated using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model.

For the dispersion test, a simple sonication-precipitation method was utilized for this study. Briefly, 0.5 mg of PRB coal or TR powder were weighed and placed in a 20-mL vial. Then, 10 mL of a solvent was added, and the solution is sonicated for 30 mins. The solvents used for this study were DI water, ethanol, acetone, DMF, and DMSO. Photographs were taken intermittently at different times (0, 1, 3, 6, and 12 h).

The viscosity of the electrospinning suspensions was evaluated using Ares-G2 rheometer (TA Instruments). For the initial fiber morphology inspection, a laboratory microscope (AmScope) equipped with a microscope digital camera (MU1803) was used. The digital camera was fixed at 4.5x magnification enough to see the visual appearance of the as-spun fibers. High magnification and resolution images were obtained using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (FEI Quanta FEG 450).

Molecular dynamics simulations were carried out under a 1.0 V potential using graphite as our carbon electrode platform in LAMMPS molecular dynamics package. Molecular/ionic structures and initial LAMMPS data files were generated using Avogadro and Moltemplate. The simulation details are provided in the supplemental information.

Nickel foam was used as current collector and cut into two dimensions: (a) 3 cm by 1.5 cm, and (b) 1.5 cm by 1.5 cm. They were then cleaned by sonicating in the presence of 37% HCl acid, followed by DI water rinsing until pH reaches 7. Finally, these pretreated nickel foam pieces were rinsed with ethanol and dried in the vacuum oven at 60°C overnight.

To prepare the electrodes, a piece of the CNF weighing 1 mg each (∼1 cm by 1 cm) was cut from a large electrospun mat. The CNF is then sandwiched between two nickel foam pieces and pressed at 1 kg cm–2 using a hydraulic press. The prepared electrode served as a working electrode, with Pt foil as a counter electrode, and Hg/HgO as a reference electrode. The electrolyte is 6 M KOH or 6 M KOH with 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6, denoted as KOH and the mixed electrolyte, respectively. All electrochemical evaluations were characterized using CompactStat e10800 potentiostat from Ivium Technology. Briefly, cyclic voltammetry (CV), and galvanostatic charge and discharge (GCD) experiments were conducted in a potential range of -0.9 to 0.1 V in a three-electrode system. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was evaluated at 0.01 – 105 Hz.

For the two-electrode system, symmetrical supercapacitor (SSC) was evaluated in the mixed electrolyte. The detailed calculations related to electrochemical characterization are provided in the supplemental information.

To understand how physiochemical properties of TR affect the final properties of CNFs, surface characterizations were carried out using FTIR and XRD. Briefly, the FTIR peaks depict a broad range assigned for OH-stretching (3700 to 3000 cm–1), clay minerals (3696 and 3618 cm–1), CH2/CH3 stretching vibrations (2926 cm–1/ 2848 cm–1), C=O stretching (1701 cm–1), C=C stretching vibration (1588 cm–1), CH2/CH3 bending vibration (1438 cm–1/1372 cm–1), and C-O-R stretching vibrations (1090, 1035, and 1008 cm–1), and aromatic C-H and C-C out-of-plane stretching vibrations (900 to 700 cm–1) [31,35]. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 1 a) reveal that the functional groups of interest for both PRB and TR powder appear at 1701 cm–1 and 1150 to 800 cm–1, as denoted in the red line (Fig. 1a). A peak centered at 1701 cm–1, representing C=O stretching, was observed for both PRB coal and TR powder. However, this peak was reduced in the TR spectra, indicating the decarboxylation function associated with tetralin extraction, since the residue is what is left behind after removing the tetralin liquid extract. Similarly, the peaks ranging from 1150 to 800 cm–1 from various functional groups diminished. For instance, the decline in peak intensity at ∼ 910 cm–1 from PRB to TR shows the significant reduction of functional groups associated with C=C. Peaks at ∼1090 to 1008 cm–1, representing the groups of C-O and CO-O-CO stretching, respectively, also dramatically reduced.

The XRD of raw PRB and TR powders are presented in Fig. 1b. The peaks representative of raw PRB coal are consistent with open literature [29,36] showing the presence of trace minerals such as kaolinite (K), quartz (Q), magnetite (M), dolomite (D), illite (I) and siderite (S) hematite (H), and mullite (M’). Peaks from these minerals were observed to have reduced intensities in TR powder, suggesting that tetralin extraction significantly reduced these mineral contents. The dispersion comparison in various solvents (water, ethanol, acetone, DMF and DMSO) of TR vs. PRB coal are pictured in Fig. S1-S2. Visual inspection showed that the TR powder dispersed better than PRB coal in all five selected solvents (Fig. S1), with higher yield of carbon residue, fewer volatile materials, as revealed by TGA results in Fig. S2a-b. Additionally, SEM images showed rougher and more porous structure of TR powder (Fig. S2d) than PRB (Fig. S2c).

To convert TR powder into carbon fibers via electrospinning, it is essential to make a spinnable solution with desirable viscosity. PVP and PAN were chosen as polymer binder and porogen, respectively. Different amounts of TR powder were then added to the polymer solution of PVP and PAN in DMF. Both PAN (Fig. S3a) and PVP (Fig. S3b) developed a fiber mat-like structure after electrospinning, assuring that both polymers were good candidates for potentially producing carbon fiber mats. However, it was observed that PAN can survive extreme oxidation and carbonization conditions (Fig. S4-S5), whereas PVP cannot endure oxidation (Fig. S4). It is widely known that PVP is a porogen agent which yields pores, thus, altering different levels of porosity and befitting many applications [37]. Hence, the addition of PVP serves two roles: 1) to provide a solution with an appropriate viscosity to spin into fiber form, and 2) to act as a porogen. The first use is to provide a solution with an appropriate viscosity to spin into fiber form. Fig. S6 shows the viscosity obtained from representative electrospinning solutions of PAN, PVP, PAN-PVP, and PAN-PVP-20TR. Even though each solution contains 10 wt.% of solids, PAN has higher viscosity (0.6 Pa s) than PVP (0.1 Pa s). With a 5:5 wt.% ratio, PAN-PVP has a viscosity of 0.35 Pa s. As shown previously by our group [29], such a ratio prevents the formation of beaded fiber, and enables the formation of a uniform fiber network. However, from the photographs of the oxidized fiber mat (Fig. S4), PVP has shown tremendous structure loss from extreme shrinkage. Even with PAN-PVP, a significant decrease in the areal dimension has been observed. Thus, as an effective porogen, addition of excessive amount of PVP may have compromised mechanical stability of the resultant fiber.

Fig. 2 a shows the successful preparation of the representative carbonized fiber, PAN-PVP-20TR. The nitrogen adsorption isotherms of the carbonized fiber showed a typical behavior of physisorption for mesoporous materials (Fig. 2b). A strong adsorption at very low pressure was due to a high surface area available for monolayer adsorption, which is followed by an approximated linear increase in the amount of adsorbed N2 (multilayer formation according to BET model). As the pressure approaches p/p0 = 1, a steep increase in the amount of adsorbed N2 may imply condensation of N2 in very small pores. Following the desorption curve, the multilayer adsorbed gas can be removed by returning to low pressures, but the monolayer stays adsorbed, which implies strong interaction between the fiber and N2 [38].

Incorporation of TR into the PAN and PVP matrix resulted in CNFs with surface area as high as 281 m2 g–1, higher than carbon fibers prepared by liquid pulse injection technique (200 m2 g–1) [39] and surface-modified carbon fiber (46 m2 g–1) [40]. Aside from the high surface area, the pore volume was evaluated to be 0.37 cm3 g–1, with high percentage of pores in the microporous region (Fig. 2c), which has been characterized to be desirable morphology features for carbon electrodes.

To evaluate the feasibility of using the coal refining waste as a feedstock, the effect of adding different amounts of TR powder to the starting PAN/PVP spinning solution was evaluated in this study. As shown in Fig. S3, as spun fibers from PAN or PVP show typical fiber structures. With the addition of TR, small dots were observed to be integrated within the fiber structures (Fig. S8). With the increase of the amount of TR from 5 wt% (Fig. 5c. Fig. S8a) to 50 wt.% ratio (Fig. 5i, Fig. S8b), more dots appeared, signifying that higher amounts of TR powder are embedded within the fiber. Uniform jets of electrospinning solution (Fig. S7a) were observed with TR in the solution that are uniform and continuous during the fiber production (Fig. S7b). Also, the as-oxidized fibers showed less structure shrinkage with the increase of TR concentration (Fig. S4), signifying that even when carbon is lost from the degradation of PVP, the presence of TR powder may contribute to the structural integrity within the fibers. However, PAN-PVP-100TR had blotches throughout the collecting Al foil (Figs. S7d and S8c-d). It is also worth noting that for this high concentration of TR (100 wt.%) the solution was delivered at the tip of the needle as drops (Fig. S7c) instead of a jet, demonstrating a spraying behavior as shown in Fig. S7d. We postulate that at 100 wt.% ratio of TR to PAN/PVP, the viscosity of the solution is too high, resulting in noncontinuous fibers.

SEM images of the carbonized nanofibers (CNFs) (Fig. 3 ) reveal that fiber morphology evolution with the increase of TR content. Initially, fibers from PAN showed a diameter of ∼310 nm (Fig. S5), while PAN-PVP fibers showing a smaller diameter of ∼170 nm (Fig. 3b) (see zoomed in SEM images in Fig. S10 for detail). The addition of TR to the electrospinning solution has resulted in two interesting observations. First, with the increase of TR concentration from 5% (PAN-PVP-5TR) to 10% PAN-PVP-10TR) in the solution, the fiber diameter remained in the range of 205 – 220 nm (Fig. S10). When the TR concentration increased to 50% (PAN-PVP-50TR), a dramatic variation in fiber diameter was observed (86 – 288 nm, Fig. S10). Although this concentration allowed the formation of continuous fiber without disruption, Figs. 3i and 3j indeed showed presence of large particles (∼4-10 µm) in the fiber mat.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of CNFs in a three-electrode system

ce:section.ce:para: To assess the CNFs’ electrochemical performance, the electrodes were characterized using a three-electrode system in 6 M KOH. Fig. 4 a shows the CV curves of resultant CNFs, with all CNFs exhibiting rectangular CV profiles. This may be attributed to the EDLC storage mechanism for carbon-based electrodes. The Nyquist plot (Fig. 4b) showed a semicircle and a slope for all electrodes which is typical of a supercapacitor and represented by the equivalent series circuit (Fig. S11). The internal resistance contributed by the electrode resistance, electrolyte resistance, and charge transfer resistance is represented by the depressed semicircle at the high-frequency range [41]. At the low-frequency range, the slope represents the linear Warburg related to the ion transport limitation of the electrolyte to the porous electrode, indicating a dominant capacitive behavior from an electric double layer [42]. The inset of Fig. 4b shows the high magnification at a high-frequency range, revealing the slight increase of resistance with the increase of TR content. For instance, the internal resistance of PAN-PVP-20TR is 0.58 Ω, while that from PAN-PVP fiber mat is 0.56 Ω. Further increase of TR from 20% 0.58 Ω to 50% (0.72 Ω) resulted in 20% increase in internal resistance.

Fig. 4c presents the specific capacitances at different current densities, calculated using the GCD profiles in Fig. S12a-b. For the current density range of 1 to 20 A g–1, PAN-PVP-5TR exhibited the largest specific capacitance in 6 M KOH. As revealed by the EIS analysis (Fig. 4b), the decline in specific capacitance may be caused by the increase in electrode resistance with higher TR content. To further investigate the electrodes, the specific capacitance is plotted as a function of TR's content (Fig. S12c). Here, it was evident that indeed, PAN-PVP-5TR showed the highest capacitance. Particularly at the low current density of 1 A g–1, a decreasing specific capacitance trend was evident with the increase of TR content. When the current density was raised from 2 to 20 A g–1, PAN-PVP-50TR deviated away from the trend (Fig. S12c). Note that chunks of particles, only secured by thin fibers, were evident from its SEM image (Fig. 3j). Hence, the electrodes may respond differently when subjected to extreme stress. In this case, PAN-PVP-50TR was subjected to high current densities in an aqueous environment which may have resulted in the instability of the electrode. On the other hand, PAN-PVP-20TR maintained a similar profile wherein its strong and complete integration within the fibers (Fig. 3h) was secured. At a low current density of 1 A g–1, the specific capacitances (Fig. 4d) were within a similar range of value. Likewise, it was noticeable that all of the TR-containing CNFs showed higher specific capacitance (1 A g–1) than the base polymer PAN-PVP CNF, which is also supported in our previous work [29]. This may be attributed to the distinctive properties, such as unique pore structures, from incorporating coal extraction fragments, such as TR. It is known that the specific capacitance related to EDLC storage mechanism-based materials which were commonly found in carbon materials can be ascribed by the following equation:
(eq. (1))
C =
ε
A
d
Where C is capacitance; A is surface area; ε is the dielectric constant; and d is the distance between the electrode material/electrolyte ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mixed electrolyte (6 M KOH + 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6 electrolyte)
ce:section.ce:para: All the CNFs maintained a rectangular CV curve at 50 mV s–1 as shown in Fig. 5 a, after adding 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6 to 6M KOH electrolyte. No additional redox peaks were observed on the CV curves. The Nyquist plot (Fig. 5b) showed a depressed semicircle and a slope for all electrodes, as with 6M KOH, but with the semicircle being more depressed, revealing higher resistance with the increase of TR content in the fiber mat. Additionally, the incorporation of redox additive increased the resistance of all CNFs, with the internal resistance of PAN-PVP, PAN-PVP-20TR, and PAN-PVP-50TR being 0.63, 0.84, and 1.23 Ω, respectively. The GCD profiles at different current densities (Fig. S13a-b) showed significant increase of the specific capacitance of all CNFs with the addition of K3Fe(CN)6 (Fig. 5c). At 1 A g–1, specific capacitance for PAN-PVP-5TR increased to 395 F g–1, which is a 150% increase in capacitance from that in 6M KOH (262 F g–1). With TR concentration increase from 5% to 20%, PAN-PVP-20TR showed the specific capacitance of 658 F g–1 which also exhibited superior performance to other reported carbon fiber and coal-related supercapacitor electrodes in three-electrode systems (Table S2). To summarize, the specific capacitance increased by ∼1.3, 1.5, 2.1, 2.6, and 2.0 times for 5%, 10%, 20% and 50% of TR (wt.% ratio to the sum of PAN/PVP) with the addition of 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6, compared to fibers without TR (PAN/PVP) .

The specific capacitance as a function of TR content is plotted in Fig. S13c. Unlike the response in 6M KOH only eletrolyte (Fig. S12c), PAN-PVP-20TR exhibited an interesting performance for current densities of 1 to 3 A g-1 with the 0.01M K3Fe(CN)6 additive. At these current densities, PAN-PVP-20TR clearly outperformed PAN-PVP-5TR. However, when current densities increased further (5 to 20 A g–1), a decline in the specific capacitance occurred for PAN-PVP-20TR. It is worth pointing out, however, that the lowest with the additive is still higher than the highest value in only 6M KOH. Intriguingly, PAN-PVP-50TR showed a similar trend in the mixed electrolyte to that in only 6M KOH for the higher current density range of 10 – 20 A g–1. Clearly, the effect of the 0.01M K3Fe(CN)6 additive on electrode performance is nonlinear. While future in-situ electrolyte-electrode interface study may shed light on such intriguing observation, it is evident that PAN-PVP-20TR is shown to be an stable electrode in both 6M KOH eletrolyte (Fig. S12c) and in the mixed electrolyte (Fig. 5c-d) at current density of 1 A g–1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between the two electrolytes coupled with theoretical simulations
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the role of K3Fe(CN)6 in dramatically improving capacitance, the fibers with the highest specific capacitance, PAN-PVP-20TR, was selected for the comparative analysis for KOH and mixed electrolyte (Fig. 6 ). First, it is worth noting that the CV of the same CNF electrode showed a larger integrated area in the mixed electrolyte than that in 6M KOH (Fig. 6a). A higher current was achieved in the forward scans towards both upper and lower voltage in the mixed electrolyte. This increased current between alkaline electrolyte and redox modified mixed electrolyte by K3Fe(CN)6 was also observed in a sodium ion supercapacitor (NaMnPO4) [43]. In comparison, the GCD profiles using the mixed electrolyte showed longer charge and discharge time (Fig. 6b). Additionally, the GCD for the mixed electrolyte are not the typical triangular profile as in KOH. Instead, it exhibits a pseudocapacitive-like behavior. As highlighted by Fig. 6c, the ratio of discharge over charge time increased from 1.57 (6 M KOH) to 2.06 (mixed electrolyte). While the charge curves were also prolonged, the discharge profiles last longer in the mixed electrolyte than in KOH alone.

To determine the type of storage mechanism present in the electrochemical cell, the power-law was used to evaluate the b value as shown in the following equation:
(eq. (2))
i =
a ·
v
b

Where the relationship of the current (i, mA) to scan rate (v, mV s–1) is plotted, and a and b are both constants. If the b value is equal to 0.5, the storage mechanism involves a diffusive contribution. If the b value reaches 1, it relays the dominant capacitive contribution. The b values are then incorporated back into the equation (Eq. (2)) and fitted to the capacitive/diffusive-dominated distribution along the original CV curve [44]. As shown in Fig. 6d, 98.7% of the PAN-PVP-20TR electrode in 6M KOH is contributed to its capacitive behavior, while the capacitive contribution increased to 99.3% in mixed electrolyte (Fig. 6e). The approximation of a rectangular-like CV with the b value approaching 1, strongly suggests the pseudocapacitive nature of the electrode [10,45]. As reported, carbon electrodes derived from coal fragments often contain quinone and pyridinic functional groups [29], which may contribute to redox activity along -0.9 V (see Fig. 6d-e). The comparison of Nyquist plots of PAN-PVP-20TR in KOH vs. the mixed electrolyte (Fig. 6f) clearly demonstrates that the internal resistance increased from 0.58 to 0.84 Ω with the addition of the redox electrolyte.

The dual role of redox-active electrolytes refers to their capability of forming electrical double-layers and surface redox activity [34]. Various reports have evaluated the ‘electron shuttle effect’ from using K3Fe(CN)6 as an additive redox electrolyte, by providing redox activities with the inclusion of redox-active metal ions, such as Cu and Co ions [32,33]. The Faradaic reaction of K3Fe(CN)6 on a capacitor surface may be described as shown in Eq. (3) [46]:
(eq. (3))
[
F e
(
C N
)
6
]
3 −
+ e −
↔
[ F e
(
C N
)
6
]
4 −

However, there are very limited reports regarding the mechanism on redox activities on carbon electrode surfaces [47,48]. Chen et al. [47] evaluated an supercapacitor system using a graphene paper electrode in mixed electrolyte of K3Fe(CN)6 in Na2SO4. Yang et al. [48] evaluated the performance of activated carbon in K3Fe(CN)6 and KOH.

The adsorption of negative ferricyanide ions on positive electrode surface not only repels OH–, but also creates a stronger attraction towards K+ than OH–. The build-up of negative charges from ferricyanide ions attracted drifting K+ ions within the diffuse layer (x2 ). Fig. 7f showed the increased K+ ion intensity in the first peak and shift to the right of the second peak. On the surface of the negative electrode, a slightly higher intensity of K+ ions were observed. However, no significant change in the ion distribution for both OH– (Fig. 7g) and K+ (Fig. 7h) was observed in both electrolytes, suggesting that the surface of the negative electrode has already been saturated by the K+ ions in the 6 M KOH before the addition of 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6.

To explore the supercapacitor application of PAN-PVP-20TR, the SSC devices were fabricated. Fig. 8 a shows the voltage range can be between 1.0 to 2.0 V. When the voltage approaches 1.8 V, a small oxidation peak appeared. The intensity of this peak increased when the voltage gets closer towards 2.0 V, which is the known challenge of carbon-based electrodes in aqueous electrolytes [52,53]. The GCD profile in Fig. 8b also shows the oxidation peak before 1.8 V. As a result, charging the SSC to 1.8 V was problematic, only achieving 1.75 V at the end of 1000 s. On the other hand, the discharge curve consists of a large potential drop, starting at 1.65 V (expecting from 1.8 V), ending the discharge profile after 74 s. This abnormal behavior limits the Coulombic efficiency (CE) to low values, which decreased further after 1.6 V. Thus, it is appropriate and safe to design the maximum potential window of the SSC device at 1.6 V.

Fig. 8c shows the GCD profile at different current densities. The specific capacitance of the SSC device is 47.3 F g–1 at 0.5 A g–1, 40.3 F g–1 at 1 A g–1, 24.0 F g–1 at 2 A g–1, and 14.1 F g–1 at 3 A g–1. The maximum energy density is calculated as 16.8 Wh kg–1 with a power density of 362.2 W kg–1. At a power density of 1871.4 W kg–1, the energy density is 5.0 Wh kg–1. The SSC from coal extraction waste in this study demonstrate comparable performance to other coal-related fabricated devices (Fig. 8d) [15,54-61].

Additionally, the device was subjected to 10,000 continuous GCD cycles (Fig. 8e). A low specific capacitance was observed for the first 1000 cycles. Based on the CE plotted within the first 1000 cycles, an unbalanced discharging time versus charging time (CE < 100%) was observed. We postulate that the SSCs may not have been fully wetted during these initial cycles. In other words, electrode/electrolyte immersion has not been reached yet. The stability and complete wetting were reached after the first 1000 cycles, when the CE has been stabilized at ∼99.13%. After the cycling, the device maintained ∼95.83% of the specific capacitance and ∼101.77% CE.

To evaluate how SSCs can expand the voltage range, two SSC devices were connected in series. The setup for charging and powering the devices is shown in Fig. S14. Briefly, a 12-V solar panel is placed near a well-lit window with full exposure to the sun. The solar panel was connected to the SSCs to demonstrate how the SSC can be charged by the solar panel. The fully charged SSC devices were then completely disconnected from the solar panel and then connected directly to 10 light-emitting diodes (LEDs) connected in parallel. Upon charging for 60 s, Fig. 8f shows the total voltage of ∼3.8 V for the two SSC devices connected in series. Both red and blue LEDs were successfully lit by the SSC device. The charged SSCs powered the red LED lights for 90 s as shown in time interval photos (Fig. S15). Our lab setup shows the feasibility of using coal converted electrodes as a cheap carbon source for electrodes in energy storage devices.

TR powder, a waste stream of PRB coal extraction process, was efficiently integrated into electrospinning solutions, resulting in the successful formation of conductive and interconnected carbon nanofibers (CNFs). The CNFs derived from TR powder showed better electrochemical performance than fibers from PAN-PVP. Among all electrodes, the PAN-PVP-20TR exhibited 251 F g–1 in the 6 M KOH electrolyte. Impressively, the addition of 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6 to 6M KOH resulted in the specific capacitance enhancement up to 658 F g–1. The significant increase in capacitance (162%) is attributed to the combined effect of pseudocapacitive and the electric double layer capacitance enhancement in the mixed electrolyte system. Our molecular dynamics modeling results reveal that the pseudocapacitive contribution originated from the preferred, direct adsorption of ferricyanide ions onto the carbon fiber electrode surface. Consequently, the ferricyanide ion layer enhanced the overall capacitance arising from the redox activity on the porous carbon electrode. A prototype symmetric supercapacitor (SSC) was fabricated with a 1.6 V rating, demonstrating competitive cycling stability (∼95.83% at 10,000 cycles), and an energy density of 16.8 Wh kg–1 at a power density of 362.2 W kg–1. Together with our previous body of work in this area, this study further showed that powder river basin (PRB) coal can be converted into effective carbon materials via electrospinning process for electrochemical energy storage devices, for a wide range of extraction solvents. As we gradually retire coal power plants, our systematic study paves the road to use PRB coal as an abundant feedstock for carbon materials in electrochemical energy storage sections.

Charmaine SJ Lamiel: conceptualization, methodology, original draft preparation. Mohammad M. Afroz: MD modeling and original draft preparation related to MD. Robert EF Cincotta: Rheology and SEM data collection. Maximillian Krödel: carbon fiber and TR powder characterization. Katie D. Li-Oakey: conceptualization, supervision, writing, reviewing and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We sincerely thank School of Energy Resources (SER) at University of Wyoming for funding this project and Professors Joseph Holles at Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Wyoming, and Christoph Müller at Laboratory of Energy Science and Engineering, Department of Mechanical and Process Engineering, ETH, Zürich, for allowing us to use their physisorption instruments to characterize CNF porous structures.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2023.142076.
